meta.title: The Verdict in the Applause
meta.theme: how a critic reads standing ovations as a claim about audiences, not about plays

format.format_verified: 2026-07: five sentences, TITA, +3 with no negative marking, consistent in released papers 2016 to 2025; re-verify against each new official paper

traps.description: Trap B: the outlier is given a pronoun and a shared keyword so it hooks locally onto a neighbour, while its stance shifts from the author's argument to a plain report of public opinion.

meta.learning_objective: notice when a sentence swaps the author's argument for a neutral report of what people think

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General cultural sociology of theatre audiences; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Odd One Out milestone); ODD_MAN_OUT_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Medium, first Trap B. The core is a critic's argument that ovations measure audiences, not plays. The outlier reports, neutrally, that many theatregoers enjoy giving ovations, hooked on by a pronoun and the word ovation. Stance shift from argument to reportage. Outlier at C. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: The standing ovation, the critic argued, has become so routine that it now measures the audience's mood far better than the play's merit.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: A crowd that has paid a great deal and travelled a long way is, he noted, strongly inclined to rise, if only to justify the evening to itself.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: Surveys find that most theatregoers say they enjoy taking part in an ovation, whatever they privately thought of the performance.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: Once a few near the front stand, moreover, those behind rise almost against their will, unwilling to be the only ones still seated.

sentences.label: E
sentences.text: By the time the whole house is on its feet, then, the gesture has certified nothing about the stage and a great deal about the seats.

builder.prompt: Build the critic's four sentence argument that the ovation measures the audience, not the play. The sentence left out is your answer.

explanation.spine_note: The four sentences build one argument in the critic's voice. A states the claim, that the ovation measures the audience rather than the play. B and D give two reasons the audience rises anyway, the cost of the evening and the pressure to conform. E concludes that the whole house standing certifies the seats, not the stage. Claim, two supports, conclusion.

core_roles.label: A
core_roles.why_here: It states the critic's thesis, and the phrase the critic argued marks the whole paragraph as his case, not a survey report.

core_roles.label: B
core_roles.why_here: It gives the first reason the audience rises regardless of merit, the sunk cost of a long, expensive evening.

core_roles.label: D
core_roles.why_here: It adds the second reason with moreover, the social pressure of a rising crowd, stacking support under the thesis.

core_roles.label: E
core_roles.why_here: It closes the argument with then, drawing the thesis to its point: the ovation certifies the seats, not the stage.

links.explanation: B's he noted keeps the critic's voice from A, and the crowd it describes is the audience A's thesis is about.
D opens with moreover, explicitly adding a second reason to the first, both supporting the same claim.
E opens with then, gathering both reasons into the conclusion the argument has been driving toward.

explanation.violation_named: Sentence C is a stance shift. The core is the critic's argument, a case with a claim, reasons and a conclusion, all in his evaluating voice. C drops that voice for a flat report of survey findings about what theatregoers say they enjoy. It describes public opinion where the paragraph argues against the meaning of that opinion.
explanation.why_it_breaks: The critic's argument needs each sentence to push toward the conclusion that ovations are worthless as verdicts. C simply records that people like ovations, which neither supports nor advances that claim; it merely reports a fact about preferences. B and D explain why people rise despite the play. C explains nothing; it is reportage sitting inside an argument, and the argument has no seat for a sentence that only describes.
explanation.trap_exposed: C is built to look like it belongs. It shares the exact word ovation with A and E, and its clause whatever they privately thought echoes the core's theme that applause hides real opinion. Those hooks are the bait. But look at the voice: A, B, D and E are all the critic reasoning toward a verdict, while C is a neutral survey result with no verdict in it. The keyword matches; the stance does not.

exclusion_analysis.label: A
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It reads as a broad opening generalisation, so a reader deciding early might treat it as a frame rather than the thesis.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without A the paragraph has no claim: B and D give reasons for a conclusion that was never stated, and E's certified nothing lands on nothing.

exclusion_analysis.label: B
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It flows so naturally into D's crowd behaviour that the two can feel like one unit, tempting a reader to cut the standalone looking one.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without B the argument loses its first and strongest reason, the sunk cost, and rests on conformity alone.

exclusion_analysis.label: D
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Its opening moreover makes it feel like an add on, as though the argument were already complete without it.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without D the second reason is gone, and E's whole house on its feet arrives with no explanation of how the standing spread.

exclusion_analysis.label: E
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Its epigrammatic close, about the stage and the seats, has a polished register that stands apart from the plainer reasons.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without E the argument states its claim and its reasons but never lands its point, leaving the case unresolved.

explanation.detection_habit: Fix the paragraph's voice before judging any sentence. When four sentences argue a case and one merely reports what people think, the reporter is standing in the wrong paragraph, however familiar its words.

mentor.challenge: One sentence here quotes a survey. The others make a case. Notice which voice each sentence speaks in before you decide.
mentor.takeaway: Shared keywords are not shared stance. An argument cannot use a sentence that only describes.
